key_insight: A dual-domain neural network can infer crystal-surface chirality far better from momentum-space Fermi-surface maps than from atomic-structure images, revealing handedness through relative geometric orientation in reciprocal space.
engineering_relevance: This enables practical, experimentally transferable chirality detection for catalytic, sensing, and spintronic metal surfaces, with strong promise for robust CISS-oriented materials screening.
method: Train a ResNet18 classifier on labeled surface images in two domains: real-space atomic models and reciprocal-space photoemission maps.
Fine-tune the model on simulated data and transfer it to synchrotron-acquired experimental Fermi-surface projections.
Interpret chirality from orientation cues: kink-site geometry in real space and Fermi-surface polygon alignment relative to the surface normal in reciprocal space.
evidence_signal: The model reaches about 73% accuracy on atomic-structure images and about 99% on Fermi-surface projections, and transfers to experimental images with fine-tuning on just two labeled frames.
watch_item: The experimental transfer result is based on very limited labeled fine-tuning, so broader robustness across materials, noise levels, and measurement conditions still needs validation.
paper_title: Decoding Crystallographic Surface Chirality with Machine Learning: From Atomic Geometry to Fermi Surface Projections
